content:
Member-only story

How to use a specification of current behavior as an enforced contract in legacy code modernization — and when reverse-spec starts adding risk.

13 min read
1 hour ago

--

SDD gets associated with greenfield — a clean project built from scratch, where the spec drives the code. But you unlock its biggest value where the code is oldest and most abundant. Anyone who’s touched a fifteen-year-old monolith knows the moment: you change one line, and production breaks on behavior nobody remembers — because the author left three years ago, and there are no tests.
I’ve watched two refactors of the same kind end very differently. The first went in blind — the team rewrote the module, trusting they understood what it did. A week later, production broke on an undocumented edge case nobody had reproduced. The second started with an hour spent describing current behavior in the area of change — and it shipped without incident.
The difference wasn’t code quality. It was sequence. Over the next ten minutes, you’ll see why the sequence “spec what is, then spec what should be” cuts modernization risk — and exactly where that same specification starts adding risk instead.
The One Takeaway for…
